metadata.title: Why Losing One Repair Gene Creates a Target the Driver Gene Never Could
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Biology candidate 06. Mechanism: synthetic lethality — BRCA2 loss + PARP inhibition = selective tumor kill via HR deficiency.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = HR-intact / normal cell / survives; CRIMSON #BF3339 = HR-deficient / tumor cell / dies. GOLD = editor's-pen highlight once. Never swapped mid-film.
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full HR mechanism with RAD51 biochemistry, NO NHEJ mechanism detail, NO resistance mechanisms (reversion mutations), NO HRD scoring or clinical biomarker details, NO BRCA1 vs BRCA2 differences. Mechanism only: SSB -> DSB -> HR rescues (normal) vs HR absent -> die (tumor). Illustrative numbers (1000 cells) labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B20: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A cancer drug doesn't target the cancer cell's growth engine. It exploits a repair pathway the cancer cell no longer has — while every healthy cell in the body still does.

card.copy: Why losing one repair gene creates a target the driver gene never could

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow · repair-gene-as-target

beats.narration_text: A woman with ovarian cancer has her tumor sequenced. No driver in a growth pathway — but BRCA2, a DNA repair gene, is completely lost. Her oncologist prescribes olaparib.

props.headline: A woman with ovarian cancer has her tumor sequenced. No driver…

beats.scene_description: A pathology lab or oncology consultation scene — a clinician reviewing a genomic sequencing report pinned on a light board or screen, with a tumor biopsy dish in the foreground; desaturated editorial print treatment on newsprint ground
beats.image_prompt: B02, oncologist reviewing a printed genomic sequencing report in a clinical consultation room, close-up on the report with 'BRCA2: biallelic loss' highlighted, a biopsy specimen in a tube nearby, warm clinical lighting, desaturated editorial collage on newsprint ground
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] oncologist reviews sequencing report — BRCA2 loss

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [PHOTO] oncologist reviews sequencing report — BRCA2 loss

beats.narration_text: Olaparib blocks PARP — an enzyme that patches single-strand breaks in DNA. Block PARP and those breaks pile up. During replication they collapse into double-strand breaks. Every cell in her body gets the same damage.

production_viz.label: PARP blocked -> SSBs pile -> DSBs form
production_viz.mechanic: a DNA strand with PARP enzyme drawn as a small chip blocked by an X; SSB marks accumulate as crimson nicks; replication fork approaches, nicks convert to DSBs shown as clean breaks; a label 'same damage, every cell'
production_viz.note: show the shared lesion — both cell types receive identical DSBs

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] PARP blocked, SSBs to DSBs — shared by all cells

beats.narration_text: Blocking PARP should create double-strand breaks in every cell equally. Here it kills the cancer cells but spares normal cells. Why does the same drug kill one cell type and not another?

card.copy: Blocking PARP should create double-strand breaks in every cell equally. Here it kills cancer cells but spares normal cells.
card.dek: Why does the same drug kill one cell type and not another?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION — same drug, opposite fates

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION — same drug, opposite fates

beats.narration_text: To answer that, you need to know what cells do with a double-strand break. DNA has two main repair systems. Homologous recombination — the high-fidelity one — uses the sister chromatid as a perfect template to rebuild what was there.

production_viz.label: DSB repair fork — HR vs NHEJ
production_viz.mechanic: a DSB shown center; two arrows branch: left labels 'Homologous Recombination — high fidelity — uses sister chromatid' in teal; right labels 'NHEJ — error-prone — no template' in slate; HR branch shows a clean repair outcome, NHEJ shows a jagged scar
production_viz.note: establish the two pathways clearly before the BRCA2 reveal; NHEJ gets slate not crimson — it is not the villain yet

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] DSB repair fork — HR (teal, clean) vs NHEJ (slate, imprecise)

beats.narration_text: In a normal cell, BRCA2 is one of the key proteins that orchestrates HR. When a double-strand break forms, BRCA2 helps load the repair machinery onto the break. Homologous recombination runs, and the break is fixed precisely.

production_viz.label: normal cell: BRCA2 present -> HR succeeds
production_viz.mechanic: a DSB in a teal-outlined cell; a chip 'BRCA2' drops in, repair machinery assembles, the break closes into a clean teal strand; label 'HR succeeds · cell survives'
production_viz.note: teal = good/kept; one shape motion — the break closing

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] normal cell — BRCA2 present, HR closes DSB

beats.narration_text: The cancer cell has lost BRCA2. The same double-strand break forms. The cell reaches for HR — but BRCA2 is gone. Without it, HR can't run. The only fallback is NHEJ, which stitches ends together without a template.

production_viz.label: tumor cell: BRCA2 lost -> HR fails -> NHEJ fallback
production_viz.mechanic: a DSB in a crimson-outlined cell; a chip 'BRCA2' shown as an X (missing); HR attempt shown as dashed arrows that dissolve; NHEJ assembles instead with a jagged misjoined break — crimson scar; label 'NHEJ misjoins ends · chromosomal damage accumulates'
production_viz.note: crimson = bad/broken; the X on the BRCA2 chip is the key visual

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] tumor cell — BRCA2 absent, HR fails, NHEJ misjoins

beats.narration_text: This is synthetic lethality. Two defects that are each survivable alone become lethal in combination. BRCA2 loss alone — the cell survives; other repair handles routine damage. PARP inhibition alone — HR fixes the DSBs in a normal cell. But both together kill only the cell that has both.

production_viz.label: 2x2 synthetic lethality grid
production_viz.mechanic: a 2x2 grid: columns = PARP active / PARP inhibited; rows = BRCA2 intact / BRCA2 lost. Top-left: alive (teal). Top-right: alive (teal — HR rescues DSBs). Bottom-left: alive (teal — other repair handles SSBs). Bottom-right: DEAD (crimson, larger, gold highlight ring around it). Labels in each cell.
production_viz.note: GOLD = the one editor's-pen highlight on the dead corner — fill only. This is the film's central graphic.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] 2x2 synthetic lethality grid — one dead corner

beats.narration_text: The reason olaparib works isn't that it recognizes cancer cells. It can't tell them apart. What it exploits is the repair pathway they no longer have — the one normal cells still do.

card.copy: The drug does not see cancer. It exploits what the cancer lost.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] section card — mechanism pivot

beats.narration_text: So what does this look like in practice? Start with 1000 tumor cells and 1000 normal cells. The patient takes olaparib. PARP is blocked across the board — identical SSBs, identical DSBs, in every cell.

production_viz.label: 1000 tumor + 1000 normal, drug added
production_viz.mechanic: two isotype grids side by side — left: 1000 crimson squares (tumor cells), right: 1000 teal squares (normal cells). Labels: 'tumor (BRCA2 lost)' left, 'normal (BRCA2 intact)' right. Below each, a small DSB mark appears in every cell.
production_viz.note: illustrative numbers — labeled as such in FACTCHECK. Grid establishes the parallel populations.

beats.narration_text: Normal cells: HR loads onto each DSB, using the sister chromatid as a template. The break closes cleanly. Every normal cell survives.

production_viz.label: normal cells — HR repairs DSBs, all survive
production_viz.mechanic: the teal isotype grid: a teal 'HR repair' chip sweeps across the grid; each cell gains a small teal check mark; final count label '1000 / 1000 survive' in teal

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] normal cells — HR active, all 1000 survive

beats.narration_text: Tumor cells reach for the same repair. BRCA2 is absent. HR stalls. NHEJ assembles misjoined fragments. Chromosomal damage accumulates across division after division — until each cell hits the point of no return. The tumor cells die.

production_viz.label: tumor cells — HR fails, NHEJ misfires, cells die
production_viz.mechanic: the crimson isotype grid: a crimson X chip sweeps across; cells fade to dark/out one by one (FadeOut per cell); final count label 'approx 0 / 1000 survive' in crimson
production_viz.note: illustrative — labeled in FACTCHECK. This is the resolution beat.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] tumor cells — NHEJ disaster, cells die out

beats.narration_text: Same drug. Same dose. Same DNA damage in every cell. One population gone. One population intact. The difference is a single missing gene — and what that gene was doing for repair.

production_viz.label: end count — 0 tumor, 1000 normal
production_viz.mechanic: two panels side by side: left crimson '~0' label over an empty crimson grid outline; right teal '~1000' label over a full teal grid. SerifLabel 'same drug · same damage' spanning both panels below.
production_viz.note: illustrative numbers. The visual payoff of the mechanism.

beats.narration_text: The drug is not smarter than the cancer's growth machinery. It doesn't need to be. The cancer itself created the vulnerability — by losing a repair gene it could not afford to lose.

props.headline: The drug is not smarter than the cancer's growth machinery. It…

beats.scene_description: A dramatic close-up of a single cancer cell against a dark field — the cell visually broken, chromosomes fragmented, shown as a photomicrograph-style image; editorial desaturated treatment on newsprint ground, slow push-in
beats.image_prompt: B14, extreme close-up photomicrograph-style image of a single cancer cell undergoing chromosomal disaster, chromosomes in disarray and fragmented, dark background, high contrast, desaturated editorial treatment on aged newsprint ground
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] cancer cell in chromosomal disaster — the consequence

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [PHOTO] cancer cell in chromosomal disaster — the consequence

beats.narration_text: Olaparib and the other PARP inhibitors are now standard treatment for BRCA-mutant breast, ovarian, and prostate cancers. The therapeutic window is the tumor's own repair defect — the same defect that was making the tumor unstable all along.

production_viz.label: clinical implication — BRCA loss = therapeutic target
production_viz.mechanic: a simple two-panel comparison: left 'BRCA2 loss in tumor' labeled as 'genomic vulnerability' (crimson); right arrow to 'olaparib + BRCA2 loss = synthetic lethality' (teal outcome chip). Below: LabelChips for 'BRCA-mutant breast', 'ovarian', 'prostate' in slate.
production_viz.note: the implication beat — ties the mechanism to the real clinical outcome

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] BRCA loss as therapeutic target — clinical application

beats.narration_text: The principle generalizes. Any two gene losses that are each survivable alone but lethal together — that's synthetic lethality. Map the tumor's repair defects, and you can find the drug that exploits them.

card.copy: Synthetic lethality: map the repair defect, find the target.

beats.narration_text: Start with 1000 ovarian tumor cells, BRCA2 lost, and 1000 normal cells, BRCA2 intact. Olaparib goes in. PARP is blocked in all 2000 cells. Both populations accumulate identical single-strand breaks — those convert to double-strand breaks as cells replicate. Now watch what happens to each repair attempt. Normal cells: BRCA2 is present, HR loads, sister chromatid is the template, break is sealed. Tally: 1000 normal cells, all alive. Tumor cells: reach for HR, BRCA2 is absent, HR stalls, NHEJ misjoins the ends, chromosomal disaster accumulates over divisions. Tally: approximately zero tumor cells survive. Same olaparib. Same DSBs in every cell. One missing gene — in the cells that started broken — made all the difference.

production_viz.label: THE EXAMPLE — full walkthrough with counts
production_viz.mechanic: three-phase animation: (1) two side-by-side grids, 1000 crimson (tumor) + 1000 teal (normal), drug applied; (2) teal grid: HR chip sweeps, all survive, final count '1000'; (3) crimson grid: X chip sweeps, cells fade to dark, final count '~0'. SerifLabel 'illustrative numbers' below both grids.
production_viz.note: illustrative — all numbers labeled illustrative. This is THE EXAMPLE act. Longer beat to carry the narration.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] EXAMPLE — 1000+1000 cells, drug, repair outcomes, final tally

beats.narration_text: Blocking PARP should create double-strand breaks in every cell equally — and it does. It kills the cancer cells but spares normal cells because the cancer cell lost the repair gene that could have saved it. BRCA2 loss is survivable alone. PARP inhibition is survivable in a BRCA2-intact cell. Together, they are lethal — but only to the cell that has both.

card.copy: Synthetic lethality: two survivable defects, lethal together — only in the cell that has both.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] RECAP endcard — question restated, answer given

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] RECAP endcard — question restated, answer given
